title: Beauty Pageant

jokes.text: So everybody needs to pitch in $90... Because I ordered a beautiful bouquet of daffodils from a website after a few glasses of wine.
jokes.setup: Leslie announces everyone needs to pitch in $90 for Ron's flowers
jokes.punchline: She reveals she drunkenly overspent on an expensive website
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific joke showing Leslie's well-meaning but impractical nature. The delayed reveal works well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay everyone, I need you to pitch in $90 for Ron's flowers.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: $90? That's insane!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, I know. I may have gotten a little drunk and ordered them from this really expensive website.

jokes.text: So, Tom, I think you might be getting some daffodils too. Donna, you're definitely getting some. Jerry, I don't know, I'm not sure. Time will tell.
jokes.setup: Leslie admits to drunk flower ordering
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals she apparently ordered flowers for multiple people with varying certainty
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the drunk ordering premise with perfect Leslie delivery - matter-of-fact about her chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I may have ordered flowers for Ben, Tom, and Jerry while I was drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm pretty sure about Ben and Tom, but Jerry... I'm like 60% sure I ordered flowers for Jerry.

jokes.text: The girls from Talent and Poise are gonna be there? - What? - Talent and Poise. It's a strip club by the V.A. Hospital.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions contestants will be chosen for 'talent and poise'
jokes.punchline: Tom misunderstands, thinking it's a strip club name
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke with perfect character specificity. Tom's sleazy misunderstanding is perfectly in character and the setup/payoff timing is flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're looking for contestants with talent and poise.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Talent and Poise? Is that a strip club?

jokes.text: No, what's disgusting is the Glitter Factory. Do not go to the Glitter Factory.
jokes.setup: Discussion of inappropriate strip club
jokes.punchline: Tom escalates by warning about an even worse establishment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that builds on the previous joke while staying true to Tom's character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, that place is disgusting. You know what's worse though? There's this other club called the Bulge. It makes that last place look like a Sandals Jamaica.

jokes.text: So that awesome girls like you, who are not, you know, classically hot, can be rewarded for their intelligence and savvy.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to encourage April about the pageant
jokes.punchline: She accidentally insults April while trying to be supportive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her good intentions creating awkwardness through poor word choice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, you're going to be amazing in the pageant! You have such a unique look - it's so unusual and interesting!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Did you just call me ugly?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No! I'm saying you're... you're not conventionally pretty, but in a good way!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm making this worse, aren't I?

jokes.text: The coffee's $7.
jokes.setup: April brings Leslie coffee and asks for favoritism
jokes.punchline: After Leslie's ethical speech, April deadpan asks for payment
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan delivery that deflates Leslie's earnest moment. Great economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I brought you coffee. Now can you do me a favor?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, I can't show favoritism. As director of Parks and Recreation, I have to treat everyone equally and fairly.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Okay. How much do you want?

jokes.text: Just bump that clown. Tell him they already have an Asian judge.
jokes.setup: April wants Leslie to help her win
jokes.punchline: April suggests Leslie use racist reasoning to get Tom kicked off
jokes.explanation: Dark humor typical of April's character but relies on offensive stereotype for the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Leslie, I need you to help me win this thing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Of course! What do you need?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Use racist reasoning to get Tom kicked off the team.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No! April, I can't do that.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why not? It works.

jokes.text: But if you don't do it to look at women in bikinis and assign them numerical grades, what the hell do you call in favors for?
jokes.setup: Tom explains how he became a pageant judge
jokes.punchline: He's completely honest about his shallow motivations
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tom's unashamed superficiality with solid delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I became a pageant judge because I wanted to be around beautiful women and have an official title that sounds impressive at parties.

jokes.text: The original title of this was 'a lively fisting'. But they had to change it. For obvious reasons.
jokes.setup: Dave explains a historical mural depicting a fight
jokes.punchline: The innocent historical term has unfortunate modern connotations
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay joke that works on multiple levels, though somewhat disconnected from character.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: This mural depicts a historical fight between two groups. The term we use to describe it is 'a group of people beating the crap out of another group of people,' which, yes, has some unfortunate modern connotations.

jokes.text: It's Madeleine Albright. - That's her name? I call mine nana. - No, that's Madeleine Albright. First female Secretary of State.
jokes.setup: Dave sees Leslie's Madeleine Albright photo
jokes.punchline: He thinks it's Leslie's grandmother and calls his own 'nana'
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy showing the intellectual gap between Leslie and Dave through a simple misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Is that your grandmother?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's Madeleine Albright.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, she looks like my nana. Let me call her.

jokes.text: I mean, could you date someone who doesn't love giving vaccinations? - I've never dated anyone who loves giving vaccinations.
jokes.setup: Leslie compares dating incompatibility to professional interests
jokes.punchline: Ann points out the absurdity of Leslie's specific example
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor showing Leslie's tendency to overthink through Ann's practical response.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's like if you were dating someone who didn't care about government. You'd be like, 'How can we possibly make this work?'

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, most people don't care about government.

jokes.text: Well, I'll see you tonight for the weirdest second date ever.
jokes.setup: Mark offers to fix Ann's shower
jokes.punchline: Ann acknowledges the unusual nature of having a handyman date
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor that acknowledges the situation's awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: So you're going to fix my shower?

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know how to fix showers.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: This is weird. I'm dating my handyman.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Is that a problem?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No, it's just... I've never dated my handyman before.

jokes.text: 34c, 36b, 34b, 34d. 32a? How'd you get in here? I'm kidding. You're perfect, each one of you. God bless.
jokes.setup: Tom is introduced as a judge who will be fair and forward-thinking
jokes.punchline: He immediately starts sizing up contestants by bra size
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom character moment showing his complete lack of self-awareness and inappropriate behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm going to be a fair and forward-thinking judge.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Now let me just size up these contestants by bra size.

jokes.text: Hello, I'm April Ludgate. I'm 20 years old, I like... People... Places... And things. And Pawnee is my favorite place in the world.
jokes.setup: Beauty pageant contestant introductions
jokes.punchline: April gives the most generic, unenthusiastic introduction possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan performance that subverts pageant expectations with maximum apathy. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm April. I like... things. And I do... stuff. I hope you enjoy the pageant. Or don't. Whatever.

jokes.text: I've been on Youtube.
jokes.setup: Contestant giving her accomplishments
jokes.punchline: She lists being on YouTube as a notable achievement
jokes.explanation: Simple joke about modern culture's low bar for fame, though somewhat dated now.

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: I've been on YouTube.

jokes.text: They can't all be winners.
jokes.setup: Trish gives an vapid introduction
jokes.punchline: Tom's matter-of-fact assessment
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction that undercuts the moment with blunt honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That was the dumbest thing I've ever heard, and I once listened to Donna describe her medical history for six hours.

jokes.text: Looks like we got a frontrunner.
jokes.setup: After hearing Trish's terrible introduction
jokes.punchline: The veteran judge sarcastically declares her the frontrunner
jokes.explanation: Solid sarcastic response that plays on pageant judge stereotypes.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Well, she's clearly the frontrunner.

jokes.text: She's not even twirling the baton.
jokes.setup: Tom praises Trish's baton talent
jokes.punchline: Visual reveal that she's failing at basic baton twirling
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy setup with Tom's obliviousness to actual performance quality.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Trish, that baton work is incredible. You're like a young majorette.

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: Thanks, Tom!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm serious. The way you're handling that baton... it's mesmerizing.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Natalie. I love Ritalin and have low self-esteem.
jokes.setup: April does impressions as her talent
jokes.punchline: Her 'impression' of her sister is brutally honest family dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor that's perfectly April - using 'impressions' to air family issues. Highly quotable and shocking.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: For my talent, I'm going to do impressions of my family members.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: This is my sister Kathy.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Kathy. I peaked in high school and now I just complain about my life while making no effort to change anything.

jokes.text: Women should do everything. Check out my four-color pen. Listen up while I talk about some really important stuff. Parks, parks... Michelle Obama, parks. Gay penguins, parks, sugar, parks.
jokes.setup: April doing an impression of Leslie
jokes.punchline: Perfectly captures Leslie's enthusiastic verbosity and obsessions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy that perfectly encapsulates Leslie through April's cynical lens. The 'parks, parks' repetition is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Leslie Knope, and I have a crazy idea about how we can turn this parking lot into a beautiful community garden, and I've already created a 47-page presentation with color-coded tabs, and I've named all the vegetables, and I'm going to personally ensure that every single person in Pawnee knows about it by standing outside the grocery store with a clipboard and a smile that never fades.

jokes.text: She got me. She got me good.
jokes.setup: April's devastating impression of Leslie
jokes.punchline: Leslie's good-natured acknowledgment of the accuracy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie response showing her self-awareness and good humor about her quirks.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm Leslie Knope. I have about seventy-three different binders, and I've already planned out my entire life down to the minute, including my future children's names and their birth weights.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's actually not far off.

jokes.text: I hooked your toilet up to your shower. That's what you wanted?
jokes.setup: Mark was supposed to fix Ann's shower
jokes.punchline: He's somehow connected the toilet to the shower system
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist plumbing humor that escalates the handyman date premise.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Mark, why is the toilet flushing every time I turn on the shower?

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Well, I may have connected a few pipes incorrectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: A few pipes? Mark, you somehow connected the toilet to the shower system!

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Look, plumbing is complicated. At least the water pressure is good?

jokes.text: You live down here. I can't believe this is happening. I can't be at my house and see my ex-boyfriend living in a hole in my backyard like a gopher.
jokes.setup: Ann discovers someone in her backyard pit
jokes.punchline: It's revealed to be Andy living there like an animal
jokes.explanation: Great visual comedy setup with the 'gopher' comparison perfectly capturing the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What are you doing in my pit?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is my home now.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're living in my pit?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's rent-free, it's got character, and the acoustics are incredible.

jokes.text: When you had that barbecue, I didn't come up, even though it smelled so good.
jokes.setup: Andy explains he's been trying not to bother Ann
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been living close enough to smell her food
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment combining his childlike honesty with the creepy implications of his situation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been trying really hard not to bother Ann. I've been very respectful of her space.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But I've been living close enough to smell her food, so... I think we're pretty much living together.

jokes.text: First off, I just want to say I'm a little bit surprised because I didn't think angels could fly so low.
jokes.setup: Tom says he has a real question for a contestant
jokes.punchline: He opens with a terrible pickup line instead
jokes.explanation: Clichéd pickup line that works mainly through Tom's complete inappropriateness in this context.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I have a real question for you.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Did it hurt?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: When you fell from heaven?

jokes.text: Alexis De Tocqueville called America the great experiment. What can we do as citizens to improve on that experiment?
jokes.setup: Tom's terrible pickup line approach
jokes.punchline: He follows up with a genuinely sophisticated political question
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection showing Tom's unexpected depth beneath his shallow exterior.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey, are you a government contract? Because I'd like to put in a bid for your heart.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: But seriously, what's your take on the privatization of municipal services and its long-term fiscal implications?

jokes.text: But the high birthing rate of immigrants frightens me. No offense to anyone out there, but if it were up to me and my family, I would actually call it our America and not their America.
jokes.setup: Trish attempts to answer Tom's thoughtful political question
jokes.punchline: She reveals casual xenophobia while trying to sound patriotic
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe comedy exposing casual bigotry through the character's obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What do you think about the trade agreements with China?

dialogue.speaker: Trish
dialogue.line: Well, I think America should always put America first. We need to make sure our jobs stay here and not go to... you know, those other countries where people don't even speak American.

jokes.text: Don't applaud that. Don't. She didn't answer my question.
jokes.setup: Trish gives a xenophobic non-answer
jokes.punchline: Tom is more upset about her not answering than her bigotry
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Tom's priorities and intellectual vanity over moral outrage.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You didn't actually answer the question. You just said a bunch of stuff that had nothing to do with what I asked. That's what bothers me. Not the bigotry—I can work with that. But the non-answer? Come on.

jokes.text: I peeked. I also didn't see any toothpaste. Do you not use toothpaste?
jokes.setup: Mark mentions looking in Ann's medicine cabinet
jokes.punchline: He admits to snooping and asks about her dental hygiene
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor about date boundary violations, though somewhat generic.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Yeah, I looked in your medicine cabinet.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Mark!

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I was snooping. But I have to ask — do you ever floss?

jokes.text: What? We don't get cash? This is for a fence? - It won't cover a whole fence, but it'll defray the cost considerably.
jokes.setup: Prize announcement for Miss Pawnee pageant
jokes.punchline: The '$600 prize' is actually store credit for fencing supplies
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor about small-town pageant economics with perfect misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: The winner of the Miss Pawnee pageant will receive a $600 prize!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's store credit for fencing supplies.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. I quit.
jokes.setup: Learning the prize is fencing supplies
jokes.punchline: April immediately abandons the competition
jokes.explanation: Perfect April response with maximum economy - she has no reason to pretend once money is off the table.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Wait, the prize is fencing supplies?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm out.

jokes.text: No, I didn't win. But at least I didn't make any new friendships.
jokes.setup: April leaving the pageant
jokes.punchline: She finds the silver lining in avoiding human connection
jokes.explanation: Brilliant April line that perfectly captures her antisocial worldview. Highly quotable and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not upset about leaving the pageant. I'm actually relieved. Now I don't have to smile at strangers or pretend to care about their opinions. I can go back to my regularly scheduled programming of avoiding human contact.

jokes.text: Trish will win this pageant over my dead body.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to convince other judges
jokes.punchline: She dramatically stakes her life on preventing Trish from winning
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie escalation showing her tendency toward dramatic overstatement for causes she believes in.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm staking my life on this. Trish cannot win. I will literally die if she wins.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That seems like an overreaction.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious. If Trish wins, I'm done. Finished. Dead to the world.

jokes.text: I packed a suitcase.
jokes.setup: Jessica is asked what her talent was when she won
jokes.punchline: Her 'talent' was an extremely mundane life skill
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery highlighting the absurdity of beauty pageant 'talents' with maximum economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: My talent was... organizing a filing system.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: I showed them my color-coded folders and alphabetical tabs.

jokes.text: I don't know about you, Mark, but I've seen a ton of porn, and I know what fixing your shower means.
jokes.setup: Mark is at Ann's house fixing her shower
jokes.punchline: Andy innocently references porn tropes about repair visits
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment showing his naive honesty creating uncomfortable situations.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey, is Mark here? I heard there was a problem with the shower.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's in the bathroom fixing it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh cool. So like... is he gonna need any help? Because I've seen this exact scenario play out before. Usually ends with someone getting very wet.

jokes.text: A good chef never reveals her secrets. It's hot sauce.
jokes.setup: Mark asks about Ann's cooking secrets
jokes.punchline: She immediately reveals the 'secret' after saying she won't
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection joke with excellent timing and economy showing Ann's practical nature.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: What's your secret to making such great food?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Well, I could tell you, but then it wouldn't be a secret anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: It's butter. I just use a lot of butter.

jokes.text: I was thinking that there was once a time when Mark used to be the stranger in the house. And now it's me.
jokes.setup: Andy observing the dinner situation
jokes.punchline: He makes the situation more awkward by pointing out the role reversal
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy where Andy's emotional honesty makes everyone uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying that I'm the one who's supposed to be smooth and charming right now, and you're the one freaking out? That is the weirdest role reversal I've ever seen.

jokes.text: This isn't the first time that Susans have lost to Trishes. And it won't be the last.
jokes.setup: Leslie conceding the pageant results
jokes.punchline: She frames it as an eternal struggle between substance and superficiality
jokes.explanation: Excellent Leslie moment turning personal defeat into universal social commentary. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fine. Fine, you win. But let me tell you something about this pageant. This isn't about a sash or a crown. This is about the eternal struggle between substance and superficiality. And unfortunately, superficiality won today. But mark my words, the pendulum will swing back. People will realize that what matters is character, intelligence, and a genuine commitment to public service. Not... spray tans and hair extensions. So congratulations. You won a pageant. I'll be winning at life.

jokes.text: No, dummy, just you.
jokes.setup: Andy assumes he and Mark are both leaving
jokes.punchline: Ann clarifies she only wants Andy to leave
jokes.explanation: Clean reaction beat that clarifies Ann's feelings efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So we're both leaving?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No, just you.

jokes.text: That went really well! We had dinner, I got to see her. Oh, God, I was so tempted to look back at her. Was she looking? I had to walk so slow. A good day.
jokes.setup: Andy getting kicked out of Ann's house
jokes.punchline: He's completely oblivious to how badly it went
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment showing his delusional optimism in the face of clear rejection.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, you need to leave. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wow, okay! That was amazing! We should do that every night!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No, Andy, you don't understand. I'm breaking up with you.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Breaking up? I thought we were just getting started! This is the best day ever!

jokes.text: It's officer John McClane. Welcome to the party, pal.
jokes.setup: Dave arrives at the office
jokes.punchline: Leslie greets him with a Die Hard reference he won't understand
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Leslie's continued cultural references that go over Dave's head.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Dave! Welcome to the party, pal.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Thanks, Leslie.

jokes.text: Will Sandra Day O'Connor and Michelle Obama and Condoleezza Rice and Nancy Pelosi... Are they gonna join us? - No, they won't. - Good, 'cause I don't happen to agree with Miss Pelosi's views about the troubled assets relief program.
jokes.setup: Dave jokes about Leslie's powerful women obsession
jokes.punchline: He reveals he researched policy to impress her
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing Dave's efforts to connect with Leslie on her level.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I researched municipal zoning policy just to impress you.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's the most romantic thing anyone has ever said to me.

jokes.text: This is one of my favorite pick-up strategies. I'm constantly giving women my keys. So far, none of 'em have shown up.
jokes.setup: Andy offers Ann his house key
jokes.punchline: He admits this strategy has never worked
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - continuing a completely unsuccessful strategy with optimism.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Here, take my house key.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Why are you giving me your house key?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So you can let yourself in whenever you want. I've tried this with every girl I've ever liked, and it's never worked, but I'm nothing if not persistent.

tmdb_overview: Leslie gets to judge a beauty contest, but ends up having conflicting opinions on "the ideal woman" with the other judges. While on a date with Mark, Ann learns Andy has been living in the pit.
insight: Beauty pageant chaos yields 43 jokes in 24 minutes—Leslie's earnest incompetence drives relentless character comedy.
